topic: Compliance requirements for launching a consumer fintech lending product in Texas
subject: Compliance requirements for launching a consumer fintech lending product in Texas
jurisdiction: State of Texas (state licensing regime administered by the Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner), plus applicable federal consumer-finance law (CFPB/Dodd-Frank, TILA, ECOA, MLA)
summary:
RESEARCH, NOT LEGAL ADVICE. Launching a consumer fintech lending product in Texas means operating at the intersection of a Texas state licensing regime and federal consumer-protection law.

STATE (Texas): Consumer loans are governed by Texas Finance Code (TFC) Title 4. The two state-law paths a fintech most often uses are: (1) TFC Chapter 342 'Consumer Loans' — a regulated lender license issued by the Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner (OCCC) for small-dollar/installment loans, with statutory rate ceilings and licensing/exam obligations; and (2) the Chapter 393 'Credit Services Organizations' (CSO) / 'credit access business' (CAB) model used by many online/bank-partnerhip fintechs, where the fintech arranges a loan made by a third-party (often out-of-state) bank and charges its own fees. Both require OCCC registration/licensing, disclosures, recordkeeping, and annual reporting; the OCCC administers exams and brings administrative enforcement (injunctions, revocations, penalties) for violations. As of 2025, the OCCC is migrating Chapter 342 regulated-lender licensing to the Nationwide Multistate Licensing System (NMLS) under TFC §14.109/§342.551.

FEDERAL: Any consumer lender — including a fintech — must comply with federal law enforced by the CFPB: Truth in Lending/Regulation Z (12 CFR 1026, cost disclosures, APR), Equal Credit Opportunity Act/Regulation B (12 CFR 1002, no discrimination, adverse-action notices), EFTA/Regulation E for electronic payments, Gramm-Leach-Bliley privacy, the Military Lending Act (36% MAPR cap for servicemembers and dependents), and Dodd-Frank's UDAAP prohibition (unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts/practices). CFPB supervision reaches nonbank larger participants.

ENFORCEMENT & CASE LAW: The CFPB's two ACE Cash Express (Irving, TX) actions are the headline precedents — a 2014 $10M consent order for illegal debt-collection/cycle-of-debt UDAAP, and a 2022 suit (N.D. Tex., 3:22-cv-01494) alleging concealment of free repayment plans, which the Bureau voluntarily dismissed with prejudice on April 30, 2025. The OCCC's own enforcement docket shows steady use of injunctions and license revocations against regulated lenders and CABs. The 2025 FirstCash Military Lending Act settlement shows MLA risk for pawn/payday-style products.

RECENT/PENDING (last ~24 months): Texas OCCC NMLS migration rules adopted Dec. 26, 2025 (7 TAC §§83.301-83.404); OCCC CAB licensing & recordkeeping rule amendments out for precomment (draft posted July 15, 2026); CFPB Personal Financial Data Rights / open-banking rule finalized Oct. 22, 2024 (Section 1033); CFPB digital-payment-apps larger-participant rule finalized Nov. 21, 2024; CFPB BNPL interpretive rule (Regulation Z) issued May 31, 2024; and in 2025 the CFPB rescinded/withdrew many rules and guidance and several Chopra-era rules were nullified by Congressional Review Act joint resolutions. Two named Texas consumer-lending cases the user asked about — Looney v. Cash America and Davis v. ACE Cash Express — were searched for but no primary court opinion was located in this corpus (see key_cases notes).

BOTTOM LINE FOR A FOUNDER: Before launch you need (a) the right Texas OCCC license or CAB registration, (b) federal compliance rails for TILA/ECOA/MLA/UDAAP/privacy, (c) APR/rate-ceiling math that fits the TFC chapter you elect, and (d) a watch on the moving 2024-2026 CFPB and OCCC rule changes. This is a compliance brief, not a legal opinion; engage Texas consumer-finance counsel before going live.

regulations.name: Texas Finance Code Chapter 342 — Consumer Loans (Regulated Lender License)
regulations.requirement: A fintech making consumer small-dollar or installment loans to Texas borrowers generally needs an OCCC regulated lender license under Chapter 342 (see also 7 TAC Chapter 83). Licensees must meet application/character/financial-responsibility standards, use NMLS for application and renewal (migration in progress, per Dec. 26 2025 adopted rules), keep records, file annual reports, submit to OCCC examination, and comply with Chapter 342's statutory rate ceilings and contract requirements. Note: the retrieved statute page extracted only navigation chrome; section-level text (rate caps, fees) was not captured — confirm exact ceilings in the statute text with counsel.

regulations.name: Texas Finance Code Chapter 393 — Credit Services Organizations (CSO) / Credit Access Businesses (CAB)
regulations.jurisdiction: Texas (administered by OCCC; CSO registration with Texas Secretary of State)
regulations.requirement: Fintechs using the bank-partnership/CAB model register as a credit services organization / credit access business. A CAB contract must contain specified disclosures (incl. a no-prepayment-penalty statement per §393.201(c)), must not obtain credit for a consumer within seven days of a prior CAB loan being paid off (§393.308-type rollover restriction), keep records, and comply with OCCC CAB rules; municipalities may impose additional CAB ordinances that Chapter 393 does not preempt (§393.004). The OCCC is currently proposing CAB licensing & recordkeeping rule amendments (precomment draft posted July 15, 2026). Note: section-level statutory text beyond the HB 768 excerpts was not captured from the statute page — confirm exact §393.101-393.602 obligations in the statute.

regulations.name: Texas Finance Code Chapter 305 — Penalties and Remedies (Usury)
regulations.requirement: Chapter 305 establishes civil penalties and remedies for usury and other Finance Code violations (e.g., forfeiture of interest, penalties) applicable to consumer lenders that exceed rate ceilings. A fintech must ensure its APR/fees do not breach the applicable ceiling under the TFC chapter it elects (Chapter 342 or 303/351). Note: section-level text was not captured from the page (navigation chrome only); confirm specific penalty amounts in the statute.

regulations.name: Texas Finance Code Chapter 303 — Optional Rate Ceilings
regulations.requirement: Chapter 303 provides optional rate-ceiling elections that certain consumer lenders may use; combined with Chapter 342's specific ceilings it determines the maximum lawful rate/charges. A fintech must compute its APR against the correct ceiling to avoid usury exposure under Chapters 305/351. Note: section-level ceiling figures were not captured from the page (navigation chrome only); confirm exact ceilings in the statute text.

regulations.name: Truth in Lending Act / Regulation Z (12 CFR Part 1026)
regulations.requirement: A consumer lender must make clear, accurate cost disclosures before extending credit — amount financed, finance charge, APR, payment schedule — and provide periodic statements and billing-error rights for open-end credit; prohibitions on certain terms and practices. A May 31, 2024 CFPB interpretive rule clarified that BNPL/digital-user-account lenders are 'card issuers' subject to Regulation Z (periodic statements, billing disputes).

regulations.name: Equal Credit Opportunity Act / Regulation B (12 CFR Part 1002)
regulations.requirement: A lender may not discriminate on a prohibited basis (race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, receipt of public assistance, exercise of rights under the CCPA) in any aspect of a credit transaction; must provide adverse-action notices within the required timeframe (§1002.9), retain records (§1002.12), and may collect monitoring information (§1002.13). ECOA is a federal floor and does not preempt stricter Texas protections.

regulations.name: Dodd-Frank Act §1031/§1036 — Unfair, Deceptive, or Abusive Acts or Practices (UDAAP)
regulations.jurisdiction: Federal (CFPB-administered; applies concurrently with Texas law)
regulations.requirement: A fintech lender must not engage in unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices in offering or servicing consumer credit. The CFPB's 2014 ACE Cash Express order is the canonical example: illegal debt-collection threats, harassment, and pressuring borrowers into a cycle of debt were held to be UDAAP violations, resulting in $5M consumer refunds and a $5M civil penalty. UDAAP is the CFPB's broadest enforcement hook and applies to all consumer-finance conduct.

regulations.name: Military Lending Act (10 U.S.C. §987) — 36% MAPR cap and disclosures for servicemembers
regulations.requirement: For covered consumer credit extended to active-duty servicemembers and certain dependents, a lender must cap the military annual percentage rate (MAPR) at 36%, must not require arbitration or waivers of rights, and must make specified MLA disclosures. The 2025 FirstCash settlement (Fort Worth, TX pawn lender) shows the CFPB actively enforces the MLA and the 36% cap, including against Texas-based operators of pawn/title/payday-style products.

regulations.name: CFPB Personal Financial Data Rights Rule — Section 1033 (open banking / data portability)
regulations.requirement: Covered financial providers (including fintech lenders that hold consumer financial data) must, at the consumer's request, make personal financial data available and transfer it to another provider for free; limit data use to the requested product purpose (ban on bait-and-switch data harvesting); provide revocation and deletion rights (access ends immediately on revocation; deletion default; access max one year absent reauthorization). Compliance is phased by institution size — largest by April 1, 2026; smallest by April 1, 2030. Relevant to fintech lenders that pull or hold consumer banking data for underwriting.

regulations.name: CFPB Larger-Participant / Nonbank Supervision Authority (12 U.S.C. §5514; Dodd-Frank Title X)
regulations.requirement: The CFPB may define 'larger participants' in consumer-finance markets and subject nonbank companies meeting the threshold to CFPB supervisory examinations (in addition to enforcement). For fintech lenders, relevant larger-participant markets include consumer reporting, debt collection, student loan servicing, international money transfers, automobile financing, and (as of Nov. 2024) digital payment apps (>50M transactions/yr). A fintech lender above a market threshold must expect CFPB exams, recordkeeping, and compliance-management-system scrutiny.

cases.case_name: CFPB v. ACE Cash Express, Inc. (2014 consent order)
cases.holding: The CFPB found ACE (Irving, TX) engaged in unfair, deceptive, and abusive debt-collection and cycle-of-debt practices in violation of Dodd-Frank UDAAP; ordered $5M consumer refunds and a $5M civil penalty, and barred the abusive practices.

cases.case_name: CFPB v. Populus Financial Group, Inc. d/b/a ACE Cash Express, Inc. (N.D. Tex. 2022)
cases.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas (3:22-cv-01494)
cases.holding: The CFPB alleged ACE concealed no-cost repayment plans and improperly withdrew funds (UDAAP under the CFPA); on April 30, 2025 the Bureau voluntarily dismissed the action with prejudice and the court terminated the case — a notable 2025 retreat from a Texas payday-lender UDAAP suit.

cases.case_name: CFPB v. FirstCash, Inc. (Military Lending Act settlement)
cases.holding: FirstCash (Fort Worth, TX pawn lender) made pawn loans to MLA-covered servicemembers exceeding the 36% MAPR cap and included prohibited arbitration clauses; ordered $5M redress and a $4M fine, and must offer an MLA-compliant product or use the MLA safe-harbor screening.

cases.case_name: OCCC v. Cottonwood Financial Texas LLC (Credit Access Business enforcement)
cases.court: Texas Office of Consumer Credit Commissioner (administrative enforcement)
cases.holding: The OCCC issued a final injunction order against a registered Credit Access Business under TFC §14.202 — illustrating that the OCCC affirmatively enforces the CAB registration regime (not just licensed lenders), a direct compliance risk for fintech CABs.

cases.case_name: Looney v. Cash America International, Inc.
Davis v. ACE Cash Express, Inc.

changes.change: OCCC adopted rules (7 TAC §§83.301-83.404) migrating Chapter 342 regulated-lender licensing from ALECS to the Nationwide Multistate Licensing System (NMLS), authorized under TFC §14.109 and §342.551; a majority of licensed regulated lenders completed the NMLS transition in 2025. Directly affects how a new fintech applies for and renews a Texas regulated lender license.

OCCC released a precomment draft of Credit Access Business (CAB) Licensing & Recordkeeping rule amendments (informal precomments accepted through Aug. 5, 2026) — a pending rulemaking that would update CAB registration, recordkeeping, and (per the pawnshop parallel) likely data-security/breach requirements. Fintechs operating the CAB/bank-partnership model should track this rulemaking before it is finalized.

CFPB finalized the Personal Financial Data Rights (open-banking) rule under Section 1033 (Oct. 22, 2024), requiring covered providers to make consumer financial data portable on request with privacy limits; compliance phased by size (largest by April 1, 2026; smallest by April 1, 2030). However, subsequent CFPB signals (Dec. 2025) indicated interim final rules on Section 1033 amid funding constraints, so the effective timeline may shift — monitor CFPB rulemaking status.

CFPB finalized a larger-participant rule (Nov. 21, 2024) bringing federal supervision to the largest nonbank digital payment/wallet apps handling >50 million transactions/year (privacy, fraud, debanking). Relevant to fintech lenders that also operate payment/wallet rails.

CFPB issued an interpretive rule (May 31, 2024) clarifying that Buy Now, Pay Later / digital-user-account lenders are 'card issuers' subject to Regulation Z subpart B (periodic statements, billing-error/dispute rights). Fintechs offering BNPL-style consumer credit must build TILA/Reg Z compliance around these obligations.

In 2025, the CFPB rescinded or withdrew many final rules, proposed rules, and guidance documents issued since 2011, and several rules finalized under Director Chopra were made ineffective by Congressional Review Act joint resolutions or vacated by a court. Most notably for this brief, the Bureau voluntarily dismissed its 2022 UDAAP suit against ACE Cash Express with prejudice on April 30, 2025, signaling a more constrained 2025 enforcement posture. A fintech should not read this as a deregulation of substantive TILA/ECOA/MLA duties, but should monitor which CFPB rules remain in effect.

The CFPB reached a Military Lending Act settlement with FirstCash, Inc. (Fort Worth, TX) on July 11, 2025 — $5M redress and a $4M fine for making pawn loans to covered servicemembers exceeding the 36% MAPR cap and including prohibited arbitration clauses. Reinforces that the federal 36% MLA cap and disclosure requirements apply to Texas small-dollar/pawn/title/payday-style consumer credit and are actively enforced.

topic: Data privacy obligations for a US SaaS company serving EU customers
subject: Data privacy obligations for a US SaaS company serving EU customers
jurisdiction: European Union (primary: GDPR, ePrivacy Directive, EU-US Data Privacy Framework, EU AI Act) and United States (federal: FTC Act Section 5; state: California CCPA/CPRA)
summary: This is research, not legal advice; consult qualified counsel before acting. A US-based SaaS company that processes EU residents' personal data is pulled into EU data-protection law primarily through GDPR Article 3(2), which applies to a non-EU controller that offers goods/services to, or monitors the behaviour of, people in the Union. In practice that means the company must meet the full GDPR toolkit: a lawful basis and the processing principles (Art 5-6); transparency and the data-subject rights of access, rectification, erasure, portability and objection (Art 12-22); appointing an EU representative because it has no EU establishment (Art 27); written data-processing agreements with any sub-processors (Art 28); security of processing (Art 32); breach notification to a supervisory authority within 72 hours and to affected individuals (Art 33-34); and the Chapter V transfer regime. For the SaaS company's own tracking cookies and electronic messaging, the ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC) Art 5(3) requires informed consent before storing/accessing information on a user's terminal equipment. If the product uses AI and is placed on the EU market, the EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) adds tiered obligations: prohibited practices apply since 2 Feb 2025, general-purpose-AI model duties since 2 Aug 2025, and high-risk AI system duties from 2 Aug 2026. The hardest issue is Chapter V — getting EU personal data lawfully to US servers. There are two main routes. First, the EU-US Data Privacy Framework: the Commission's 10 July 2023 adequacy decision lets a US company receive EU data without additional safeguards IF it self-certifies with the Department of Commerce and commits to the DPF Principles (enforced by the FTC under Section 5); the decision relies on EO 14086's necessity/proportionality limits on US signals intelligence and a new Data Protection Review Court with binding remedial powers. Second, if the company cannot or will not join the DPF, it must use the 2021 Standard Contractual Clauses (Decision 2021/914) AND, per Schrems II, perform a transfer impact assessment of US law and add supplementary measures (e.g., strong encryption) — and suspend transfers if equivalent protection cannot be ensured. The Meta Ireland €1.2 billion fine (22 May 2023) shows the real-world cost of getting this wrong: the DPC found Meta infringed Art 46(1) by continuing EU-to-US transfers after Schrems II even with SCCs plus supplementary measures, ordered a transfer suspension and €1.2bn fine. On the US side, the FTC Act Section 5 (15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58) prohibits unfair or deceptive privacy/data-security practices and is the backstop for DPF commitments; and for US-resident data (e.g., California consumers) the CCPA/CPRA (Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 et seq.) requires, when acting as a service provider, a written contract limiting use to specified purposes and mandating the same level of privacy protection — conceptually overlapping with GDPR Art 28 processor duties. Binding vs. persuasive: the GDPR, ePrivacy Directive, AI Act, FTC Act and CCPA are binding statutes/regulations; Schrems II (CJEU Case C-311/18) is binding EU case law; the Meta Ireland decision is a binding supervisory-authority decision (affirmed via an EDPB Art 65 binding decision); the DPF adequacy decision and EO 14086 are binding instruments; FTC enforcement actions are agency orders. Gaps flagged: a pending 'Schrems III' challenge to the DPF adequacy decision (NOYB/Max Schrems have publicly signaled challenges) was NOT found in any retrieved primary court source (curia.europa.eu) after search — this is 'not found after search,' NOT 'confirmed none'; and the specific outcome of the DPF's first periodic review (announced to occur within a year of entry into force) was not retrieved.

regulations.name: General Data Protection Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2016/679)
regulations.jurisdiction: European Union (extraterritorial application to non-EU controllers/processors via Art. 3(2))
regulations.requirement: Art 3(2) extraterritorial scope: applies to a non-EU controller offering goods/services to or monitoring EU data subjects. Concrete obligations: lawful basis & principles (Art 5-6); transparency and data-subject rights — access, rectification, erasure, portability, objection (Art 12-22); appoint an EU representative if subject under Art 3(2) and no EU establishment (Art 27); written data-processing agreements with processors (Art 28); security of processing (Art 32); breach notification to supervisory authority within 72h and to affected individuals (Art 33-34); and Chapter V transfer rules requiring an adequacy decision, appropriate safeguards (e.g., SCCs), or a derogation before transferring personal data outside the EEA (Art 44-49).

regulations.name: EU-US Data Privacy Framework (adequacy decision adopted 10 July 2023)
regulations.jurisdiction: European Union – United States (transatlantic transfers under GDPR Art. 45)
regulations.requirement: A US SaaS company joins by self-certifying with the US Department of Commerce and committing to the DPF Principles (e.g., delete personal data when no longer necessary; ensure continuity of protection when sharing with third parties). Compliance with those commitments is enforced by the FTC under Section 5. The framework also relies on new US safeguards: intelligence access limited to what is necessary and proportionate, and a Data Protection Review Court (DPRC) that can adopt binding remedial measures (including data deletion) for EU-individual complaints. The framework is subject to periodic Commission review (first review within a year of entry into force).

regulations.name: Standard Contractual Clauses — Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914 of 4 June 2021
regulations.jurisdiction: European Union (transfers to third countries incl. the United States)
regulations.requirement: The data exporter and importer execute the modular SCCs to provide 'appropriate safeguards' under Art 46(1). Per Schrems II, the exporter must conduct a transfer impact assessment of the third country's law (esp. government access) and add supplementary measures (e.g., encryption) where the SCCs cannot otherwise be honoured; if protection cannot be ensured, suspend/prohibit the transfer. Exporters must also inform data subjects of the transfer/safeguards under Art 13/14. SCCs also apply to non-EU-established controllers caught by Art 3(2).

regulations.name: ePrivacy Directive (Directive 2002/58/EC, as amended)
regulations.requirement: Art 5(3): storing information on, or gaining access to information already stored in, a user's terminal equipment (i.e., cookies and similar tracking technologies) is allowed only with the user's consent, after providing clear and comprehensive information about the purposes of processing. Art 5(1) requires confidentiality of communications. Consent must meet the GDPR standard (freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous).

regulations.name: EU Artificial Intelligence Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of 13 June 2024)
regulations.requirement: Risk-based obligations for AI systems placed on the EU market: prohibited AI practices (Art 5) apply from 2 Feb 2025; obligations for providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models, governance and penalties apply from 2 Aug 2025; and high-risk AI system obligations (risk management, data governance, transparency, human oversight, logging) apply from 2 Aug 2026. The Act is without prejudice to GDPR data-protection law and is complementary to it.

regulations.name: Federal Trade Commission Act, Section 5 (15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58)
regulations.requirement: Prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; the FTC enforces companies' privacy and data-security representations and can seek monetary redress. A US SaaS company must not misrepresent its privacy/data practices, and its DPF self-certification commitments are enforceable by the FTC under Section 5.

regulations.name: California Consumer Privacy Act / California Privacy Rights Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 et seq.)
regulations.requirement: When a SaaS company acts as a service provider/processor, it must operate under a written contract with the business that: (1) specifies the personal information is disclosed only for limited and specified purposes; and (2) obligates the service provider to comply with the title and provide the same level of privacy protection. As a business collecting its own data it must give notice at collection, limit use to disclosed/compatible purposes, and keep retention reasonably necessary. These overlap conceptually with GDPR processor duties (Art 28).

regulations.name: Executive Order 14086 — Enhancing Safeguards for United States Signals Intelligence Activities (7 Oct 2022)
regulations.jurisdiction: United States (federal; prerequisite for EU-US DPF adequacy)
regulations.requirement: Limits US signals-intelligence access to data to what is necessary to advance a validated intelligence priority and proportionate to that priority, with privacy/civil-liberties consideration for all persons regardless of nationality (Sec 2). Establishes a redress mechanism (Sec 3): qualifying complaints from a qualifying state (incl. EU individuals) are investigated by the Civil Liberties Protection Officer, with review by a Data Protection Review Court (DPRC) that can adopt binding remedial measures and order deletion. This is the US safeguard that underpins the DPF adequacy decision.

cases.case_name: Data Protection Commissioner v Facebook Ireland and Maximillian Schrems (Case C-311/18) ('Schrems II')
cases.court: Court of Justice of the European Union (Grand Chamber)
cases.holding: Invalidated Commission Decision 2016/1250 (the EU-US Privacy Shield adequacy decision) because US surveillance law was not proportionately limited and the Ombudsperson mechanism did not provide effective judicial redress; upheld the validity of the standard contractual clauses (Decision 2010/87) but held transfers under SCCs require a level of protection 'essentially equivalent' to EU law — exporters must assess the third country's law (including government access) and add supplementary measures, and supervisory authorities must suspend/prohibit transfers where SCCs cannot be honoured.

cases.case_name: Data Protection Commission v Meta Platforms Ireland Limited (Meta Ireland €1.2 billion fine)
cases.court: Irish Data Protection Commission (lead supervisory authority), with EDPB Art. 65 binding decision (13 April 2023)
cases.date: 22 May 2023 (final decision adopted 12 May 2023; EDPB Art. 65 decision 13 April 2023)
cases.holding: Meta Ireland was found to have infringed Article 46(1) GDPR by continuing EU-to-US transfers after Schrems II on the basis of the 2021 SCCs plus supplementary measures that the DPC found did not address the risks to data subjects' fundamental rights; ordered to suspend future transfers within 5 months, to bring processing into compliance with Chapter V within 6 months, and fined €1.2 billion — the largest GDPR fine to date.

cases.case_name: FTC v DoNotPay and others — 'Operation AI Comply' (deceptive AI claims sweep)
cases.court: U.S. Federal Trade Commission (administrative enforcement under FTC Act Section 5)
cases.holding: The FTC brought five law enforcement actions against companies using AI hype or selling AI tools in deceptive/unfair ways, charging violations of Section 5 of the FTC Act; establishes that there is 'no AI exemption from the laws on the books' and that deceptive AI marketing and AI tools that enable deception are actionable — relevant to a SaaS company marketing AI features to EU/US customers.

changes.change: European Commission adopted the EU-US Data Privacy Framework adequacy decision, restoring a legal basis (under GDPR Art 45) for EU-to-US personal data transfers after the Privacy Shield was invalidated in Schrems II. US companies join by self-certifying with the Department of Commerce; FTC enforces commitments; new US safeguards (EO 14086: necessary/proportionate intelligence access + Data Protection Review Court) underpin adequacy. First periodic review within a year of entry into force.

President Biden signed EO 14086, imposing necessity and proportionality limits on US signals-intelligence access to personal data and creating a Data Protection Review Court (DPRC) with binding remedial powers for qualifying complaints from EU individuals. This US-side legal change was the prerequisite the European Commission relied on to adopt the DPF adequacy decision, and also supports SCC/BCR transfers by improving the third-country-law assessment under Schrems II.

EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689, adopted 13 June 2024) began phased application. From 2 Feb 2025 the prohibited AI practices (Art 5) and general provisions apply; from 2 Aug 2025 obligations for general-purpose AI model providers, governance, and penalties apply; and from 2 Aug 2026 the high-risk AI system obligations (risk management, data governance, transparency, human oversight, logging) apply. The Act is complementary to (not a replacement for) the GDPR. A US SaaS company placing AI systems on the EU market must comply with these tiered duties.
changes.date: 2 February 2025 (prohibited AI practices & general provisions apply)

changes.change: The FTC launched 'Operation AI Comply', announcing five Section 5 enforcement actions against companies using AI hype or selling AI tools in deceptive/unfair ways (including DoNotPay, marketed as 'the world's first robot lawyer'). Signals that there is 'no AI exemption from the laws on the books' — a US SaaS company marketing AI features must ensure its privacy/AI claims are not deceptive, on top of its GDPR/ePrivacy/EU AI Act obligations.

topic: Regulations and case law governing selling CBD cosmetics in California
subject: Selling CBD (cannabidiol) cosmetics in California
jurisdiction: California, United States (federal FD&C Act/MoCRA + California state law: Sherman Food Drug & Cosmetic Law, AB 45 Industrial Hemp, California Safe Cosmetics Act, AB 2775, and Proposition 65)
summary: RESEARCH, NOT LEGAL ADVICE. Selling CBD cosmetics in California is governed by a federal-plus-state stack. FEDERAL: The 2018 Farm Bill removed hemp (Cannabis sativa L. with <=0.3% delta-9 THC) from the Controlled Substances Act but explicitly preserved FDA authority, so CBD products must still comply with the FD&C Act. FDA does NOT pre-approve cosmetics (except color additives) and has NOT prohibited any cannabis/cannabis-derived ingredient in cosmetics by regulation; however, no CBD ingredient may make a cosmetic adulterated or misbranded (21 U.S.C. 361(a)), and any structure/function or disease-treatment claim turns the product into an unapproved new drug requiring FDA approval (section 505) - this is the basis for FDA's warning letters to CBD firms. The Modernization of Cosmetics Regulation Act of 2022 (MoCRA) added mandatory cosmetic FACILITY REGISTRATION (biennial renewal) and PRODUCT LISTING (annual updates); FDA delayed enforcement to July 1, 2024 and updated the Cosmetics Direct portal for renewals on Feb 11, 2026. CALIFORNIA: AB 45 (effective Oct 6, 2021) created the state industrial hemp/hemp-derived cannabinoid framework under the Sherman Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Law (HSC Ch. 9), with enforcement penalties. The California Safe Cosmetics Act of 2005 (SB 484, HSC 111791.5, eff. Jan 1, 2007) requires manufacturers/packers/distributors to REPORT to CDPH any cosmetic sold in California containing ingredients known/suspected to cause cancer or reproductive harm (regardless of concentration) - California is the only state with this requirement. CFFIRKA (SB 312, eff. Jan 1, 2022, HSC 111792.6) adds fragrance/flavor ingredient reporting (incl. fragrance allergens at >=0.01% rinse-off / >=0.001% leave-on). AB 2775 (HSC 110371, eff. July 1, 2020) requires professional/salon cosmetics to carry full ingredient labeling. Note: California does NOT require general cosmetic FACILITY registration with CDPH (it is voluntary), unlike the new federal MoCRA requirement. PROPOSITION 65: Cannabis (marijuana) smoke (cancer 06/19/2009; developmental toxicity 01/03/2020) and delta-9-THC (developmental toxicity 01/03/2020) are listed; CBD ITSELF is NOT listed. Safe-harbor warning regulations (27 CCR 25607.38-25607.47, adopted 2022) cover inhalation, ingestion, AND DERMAL application of THC-containing products (i.e., cosmetics) and prohibit the short-form warning. A CBD cosmetic with only 'unquantifiable' THC may not need a Prop 65 warning, but AB 45 does NOT make hemp THC 'naturally occurring' for Prop 65 purposes (the business bears the burden). Prop 65 penalties run up to $2,500 per violation per day and are enforceable by the AG, DAs, city attorneys, and private plaintiffs. ENFORCEMENT: FDA has issued many warning letters to CBD firms, including California-based Koi CBD (2019 and 2023), and California firms Latro Inc. (03/25/2025) and Pico IV Inc. (03/06/2025) in 2025 - all for FD&C Act violations (typically unapproved new drug claims). CASE-LAW GAP: No California court opinion specifically addressing CBD cosmetics was located in the research corpus after targeted searches; this is 'not found after search,' NOT 'confirmed none.' The primary enforceable actions found are FDA warning letters (federal administrative enforcement) rather than binding California court precedent. CONCRETE OBLIGATIONS for a CBD cosmetic seller in California: (1) keep THC <=0.3% dry weight to qualify as hemp; (2) register the manufacturing facility with FDA under MoCRA and renew biennially; list each cosmetic product with FDA with ingredients and update annually; (3) do NOT make disease-treatment or structure/function claims (or the product becomes an unapproved drug); (4) ensure the product is not adulterated or misbranded; (5) report to CDPH under CSCA/CFFIRKA if the product contains any reportable ingredient (incl. fragrance allergens); (6) if sold for professional use, include full ingredient labeling per AB 2775; (7) provide a Prop 65 warning if the product exposes consumers to cannabis smoke or delta-9-THC (use the 27 CCR 25607.38-47 safe-harbor warning, no short form); (8) comply with FDA cosmetic GMP guidelines; (9) if labeled 'organic,' obtain CDPH Organic Processed Product Registration and meet the 70% minimum organic content standard. RECENT/ONGOING: monitor CDPH CSCP updates (June 10, 2025 'Cosmetic Ingredients Ban' notice; March 27, 2024 Reportable Ingredients List update with EU fragrance allergen deadlines of 2026/2028; July 1, 2025 fee increases) and FDA CBD policy (Jan 2023 statement that FDA will work with Congress on a new CBD pathway; MoCRA biennial renewals now active).

regulations.name: Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018 (2018 Farm Bill), Pub. L. 115-334
regulations.requirement: Removes hemp (Cannabis sativa L. with ≤0.3% delta-9 THC dry weight, including derivatives/cannabinoids) from the Controlled Substances Act, but explicitly preserves FDA's authority to regulate cannabis/cannabis-derived products under the FD&C Act. CBD products must still comply with all other applicable federal laws.

regulations.name: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), 21 U.S.C. § 301 et seq. — cosmetic adulteration/misbranding (§§ 601-603) and drug claims
regulations.requirement: Cosmetics (including those containing CBD) are NOT subject to FDA premarket approval except for color additives; no cannabis/cannabis-derived ingredient is currently prohibited or restricted by regulation for cosmetics. A CBD cosmetic must not be adulterated (e.g., must not bear a poisonous/deleterious substance injurious under conditions of use, § 601(a)) or misbranded. If marketed with structure/function or disease-treatment claims, the product becomes an unapproved new drug requiring FDA approval (§ 505).

regulations.name: Modernization of Cosmetics Regulation Act of 2022 (MoCRA)
regulations.requirement: Cosmetic product facility manufacturers/processors must register facilities with FDA and renew biennially (FD&C Act § 607(a)(2)); responsible person must list each marketed cosmetic product with ingredients and update annually; update registration within 60 days of changes. FDA may suspend a facility's registration. Small-business exemptions exist but do NOT apply to products regularly contacting eye mucosa, injected, internal-use, or those altering appearance >24 hours. FDA delayed enforcement to July 1, 2024.

regulations.name: California Sherman Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Law + AB 45 (Industrial Hemp), Health and Safety Code Division 104 Part 5, Chapter 9 [§§ 111920-111929.5]
regulations.requirement: AB 45 (effective October 6, 2021) established California's industrial hemp and hemp-derived cannabinoid product framework under the Sherman Law, including provisions for hemp-derived CBD in cosmetics. It prohibits adulterated/misbranded cosmetics and sets THC limits and product standards for hemp products. Violations are subject to fines and penalties under HSC Article 1 of Chapter 8 (§ 111825 et seq.).

regulations.name: California Safe Cosmetics Act of 2005 (SB 484 / CSCA), Health and Safety Code § 111791.5
regulations.requirement: Effective January 1, 2007, requires manufacturers (and packers/distributors named on labels) of cosmetics sold in California to report to CDPH any product containing ingredients known or suspected to cause cancer, birth defects, or other reproductive harm from the five authoritative bodies listed in HSC § 111791.5. Reporting is regardless of concentration. California is the only state requiring such reporting.

regulations.name: Cosmetic Fragrance and Flavor Ingredient Right to Know Act of 2020 (CFFIRKA, SB 312), Health and Safety Code § 111792.6
regulations.requirement: Effective January 1, 2022, requires reporting of fragrance/flavor ingredients and fragrance allergens on 23 designated authoritative lists. CSCP consolidates reportable ingredients (updated twice annually; 6 months to comply). Fragrance allergens must be reported regardless of intended purpose; concentration thresholds: ≥0.01% (100 ppm) in rinse-off and ≥0.001% (10 ppm) in leave-on products. EU Annex III allergen deadlines of 2026/2028 apply.

regulations.name: AB 2775 — Professional Cosmetics Labeling, Health and Safety Code § 110371
regulations.requirement: A professional cosmetic manufactured on or after July 1, 2020, for sale in California must bear a label on its container satisfying all labeling requirements applicable to retail cosmetics under the federal FD&C Act and Fair Packaging and Labeling Act (i.e., full ingredient disclosure). A violation expands the scope of a crime under the Sherman Law.

regulations.name: California Proposition 65 — Safe Drinking Water and Toxic Enforcement Act of 1986 (HSC Div. 20, Ch. 6.6) + cannabis/THC safe-harbor warnings (27 CCR §§ 25607.38-25607.47)
regulations.requirement: Businesses selling products in California that expose consumers to listed chemicals must provide a clear and reasonable warning unless exposure is below the no-significant-risk level. Cannabis (marijuana) smoke is listed for cancer (06/19/2009) and developmental toxicity (01/03/2020); delta-9-THC is listed for developmental toxicity (01/03/2020). CBD itself is NOT listed. Safe-harbor warnings (27 CCR §§ 25607.38-25607.47, adopted 2022) cover inhalation, ingestion, AND dermal application of THC-containing products, and prohibit the short-form warning. Penalties up to $2,500 per violation per day; enforceable by AG, DAs, city attorneys, and private plaintiffs. A CBD product with 'unquantifiable' THC may not need a warning; AB 45 does NOT make hemp THC 'naturally occurring' for Prop 65 purposes.

cases.case_name: FDA Warning Letter — Koi CBD, LLC (two letters)
cases.court: U.S. Food and Drug Administration (federal agency enforcement)
cases.holding: FDA warned a California CBD firm for marketing CBD products with unsubstantiated disease-treatment/therapeutic claims, rendering them unapproved new drugs under the FD&C Act; products were also marketed as dietary supplements, which FDA has concluded CBD is excluded from being (§ 201(ff)(3)(B)).

cases.court: U.S. Food and Drug Administration (federal agency enforcement)
cases.holding: FDA warned Curaleaf for marketing CBD products with unsubstantiated therapeutic claims (e.g., for cancer, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, autism), making them unapproved new drugs under § 505 of the FD&C Act and misbranded; FDA cited claims that CBD 'may be effective' in treating serious diseases as a key violation.

cases.case_name: FDA Warning Letter — Latro Inc. (Pure Dozo) and Pico IV Inc. (California CBD firms, 2025)
cases.court: U.S. Food and Drug Administration (federal agency enforcement)
cases.date: March 25, 2025 (Latro Inc.) and March 6, 2025 (Pico IV Inc.)
cases.holding: FDA issued 2025 warning letters to California-based cannabinoid product firms (Latro Inc./Pure Dozo and Pico IV Inc.) for marketing cannabis-derived products in violation of the FD&C Act, continuing FDA's enforcement against firms making impermissible claims or selling products that do not meet statutory requirements.

changes.change: MoCRA cosmetic facility registration and product listing enforcement began. FDA delayed enforcement from the Dec 29, 2023 statutory deadline to July 1, 2024. CBD cosmetic manufacturers/processors must now register facilities with FDA (biennial renewal) and list products with ingredients (annual updates).

FDA updated the Cosmetics Direct portal (added REGISTRATION STATUS and RENEWAL DATE fields) to support biennial facility registration renewal under MoCRA. Facilities must renew every two years from initial registration date (e.g., a facility registered Feb 20, 2024 must renew by Feb 20, 2026).

CDPH's California Safe Cosmetics Program issued a 'Cosmetic Ingredients Ban' notice (June 10, 2025), reflecting ongoing state-level action to restrict/ban certain cosmetic ingredients. Businesses should monitor the CSCP Reportable Ingredients List and program updates for any ingredients relevant to CBD cosmetic formulations.

CDPH Food and Drug Branch implemented fee increases effective July 1, 2025, affecting the Cosmetic Safety Program (including export certificate fees for voluntarily registered cosmetic manufacturers).

CDPH California Safe Cosmetics Program updated the Reportable Ingredients List (March 27, 2024) to add newly listed EU fragrance allergens (Annex III of EU Cosmetics Regulation No. 1223/2009). Manufacturers must report these allergens by EU timelines (2026 or 2028), though early reporting is permitted. CBD cosmetics containing fragrance ingredients may be affected.

FDA announced (January 2023) that existing regulatory frameworks for foods and dietary supplements are not appropriate for CBD, and that it will work with Congress on a new pathway. This signals potential future federal CBD-specific legislation but, as of the research date, no such federal CBD cosmetic-specific law has been enacted; the current FD&C Act/MoCRA framework continues to apply.
